lead: Code Quality with PMD, SpotBugs, CheckStyle + Claude Code
contents: The Problem: “It Compiles” Is Not Enough Three Tools, Three Different Jobs CheckStyle: Consistent Code Style CheckStyle: Google Style Guide CheckStyle: IDE Integration PMD: Bad Patterns and Code Smells SpotBugs: Real Bugs in Compiled Bytecode Running All Three Together Ignoring Warnings Quality Tools as Regression Gates AI-Augmented: Claude Code Goes Beyond Rules Claude Code as Intelligent Reviewer Refactoring Without Tests Exercise 3.1: Configure Quality Tools Exercise 3.2: Bug Hunting Exercise 3.3: AI vs. Static Analysis Clean Logging: SLF4J + Log4J Instead of System.out The Vibe Coding Experiment The Data: Unconstrained AI Code Quality CLAUDE.md: Teaching AI Your Project Rules Custom Rules and Commands Built-in Tools: What Claude Code Can Do The Constrained Re-Do Exercise 3.4: Vibe Coding vs. Constrained AI Module 3: Key Takeaways

title: The Problem: “It Compiles” Is Not Enough

body.card: Code can compile and still be:
items: Hard to read
Full of hidden bugs
Inconsistent across team members
Using dangerous patterns

body.code: // Compiles. Runs. But is it GOOD? public double calc(double x, double y, int t) { if (t == 1) return x + y; if (t == 2) return x - y; if (t == 3) return x * y; if (t == 4) { if (y != 0) return x / y; else return 0; } return 0; }
body.note: Unclear names, magic numbers, silent failure on divide by zero, hard to extend.

rows: Actual bugs, null pointers, resource leaks
table.note: They complement each other. None replaces the others.

body.code: mvn checkstyle:check

// Before CheckStyle (5 developers, 5 styles): public void calcAvg(ArrayList grades,boolean verbose){ if(verbose==true){System.out.println("calculating...");} // After CheckStyle (Google Style enforced): public void calculateAverage( List<Double> grades, boolean verbose) { if (verbose) { log.info("Calculating average for {} grades", grades.size()); } }
body.note: Same logic, different readability. In a team of 20, inconsistent style makes git diff unreadable — you can’t tell what actually changed vs. what was reformatted.

title: CheckStyle: Google Style Guide
desc: The industry-standard Java style guide, enforced automatically.

body.code: <!-- pom.xml: CheckStyle with Google rules --> <plugin> <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId> <artifactId>maven-checkstyle-plugin</artifactId> <version>3.3.1</version> <configuration> <configLocation>google_checks.xml</configLocation> <failOnViolation>true</failOnViolation> </configuration> </plugin>
body.note: google_checks.xml is bundled with CheckStyle. No extra download needed.

items: Indentation: 2 spaces (not 4, not tabs)
Line length: max 100 characters
Braces: always required, even for single-line if/else
Naming: camelCase for methods, UPPER_SNAKE for constants
Javadoc: required on all public methods
Import order: static imports separated, no wildcards

title: CheckStyle: IDE Integration
desc: Format code automatically — never fix style violations manually.

items: Eclipse: Import eclipse-java-google-style.xml via Preferences → Java → Code Style → Formatter
IntelliJ: Install google-java-format plugin, or import Eclipse formatter XML
Enable Format on Save — code is always clean
Run mvn checkstyle:check to verify — zero violations expected

body.quote: No code formatting!? That costs -0.3 on your grade. Configure your IDE once, never think about it again.

title: PMD: Bad Patterns and Code Smells

body.code: mvn pmd:check

// PMD catches this: empty catch block try { int result = Integer.parseInt(userInput); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { // TODO: handle later } // Production reality: "later" never comes. // User enters "abc", app silently returns 0, // invoice is calculated wrong, client is charged $0. // Nobody knows why. No log. No error. No trace.
body.note: Each PMD rule exists because someone had a real production incident from that exact pattern.

body.card: What PMD catches that CheckStyle doesn’t:
items: Empty catch blocks — errors swallowed silently
Unused variables — dead code that confuses readers
God methods — 200-line methods nobody can review
Copy-paste code — fix a bug in one copy, forget the other

title: SpotBugs: Real Bugs in Compiled Bytecode

body.code: mvn spotbugs:check

// This code compiles. Tests pass. CheckStyle is clean. PMD is clean. // SpotBugs catches the bug: public boolean isAdmin(String username) { return username == "admin"; // BUG: == compares references, not values } // isAdmin("admin") sometimes returns true, sometimes false // depending on JVM string interning. Works in tests, // fails in production with user input. // Fix: return "admin".equals(username); // also null-safe
body.note: SpotBugs analyzes compiled bytecode, not source code. It finds bugs that are invisible to text-based tools — null dereferences, resource leaks, wrong equality checks.

title: Running All Three Together

body.code: mvn clean compile checkstyle:check pmd:check spotbugs:check
body.note: This is the command you will run hundreds of times. Memorize it.

body.code: // Bug 1: CheckStyle catches this (style) public void calcAvg(ArrayList g,boolean v){ // Wrong: no spaces, bad param names, opening brace style // Bug 2: PMD catches this (code smell) try { result = Integer.parseInt(input); } catch (Exception e) { } // empty catch block! // Bug 3: SpotBugs catches this (real bug) if (name == "admin") { // should be .equals() grantAccess(); }
body.note: Each tool catches a different class of problem. That’s why you need all three.

desc: One command, three tools, different bugs caught.

title: cognitive-core: post-edit-lint Hook
desc: What if quality checks ran automatically after every file edit?

body.code: # post-edit-lint.sh (PostToolUse hook) # Fires automatically after: Write, Edit, NotebookEdit Event: You (or Claude Code) edit Calculator.java | v post-edit-lint.sh triggers automatically | v Runs: mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check | v Result: 0 violations → silent (no interruption) 3 violations → prints warnings in context # You never need to remember to run quality tools. # The hook runs them for you. Every. Single. Time.
body.note: Configuration: CC_LINT_COMMAND in cognitive-core.conf controls which tools run. For Java: mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check.

title: Ignoring Warnings
desc: “It’s just a warning, the build still passes.”

body.card: In professional teams, warnings become errors:

body.note: Now the build fails if quality checks don’t pass. No exceptions.

title: Quality Tools as Regression Gates
desc: Quality checks that block builds follow the same pattern as AI deployment gates.

items: An automated check that runs before code can proceed to the next stage
If the check fails, the pipeline stops — no exceptions, no workarounds
Purpose: prevent code quality from getting worse over time (= regression)

body.code: # pom.xml — PMD configured as a regression gate <plugin> <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId> <artifactId>maven-pmd-plugin</artifactId> <configuration> <failOnViolation>true</failOnViolation> ← build fails if PMD finds issues <maxAllowedViolations>0</maxAllowedViolations> </configuration> </plugin>
body.note: failOnViolation: true is the key setting. Without it, PMD reports problems but the build still succeeds — and nobody reads the report.

rows: Bug patterns found (null pointers, resource leaks)
table.note: In a real pipeline (Jenkins), all these gates run automatically on every commit. One failure = build is rejected.

body.quote: A regression gate has no mercy and no exceptions. That is exactly why it works.

title: AI-Augmented: Claude Code Goes Beyond Rules

rows: “This method does three things — split it”
“This design doesn’t fit your use case”
table.note: Static analysis answers: “Does this violate a rule?” AI answers: “Is this the right design?” You need both.

body.code: You: Review Calculator.java for quality issues that PMD might miss
body.note: Static analysis tools follow rules. Claude Code understands intent.

body.code: You: Review my Calculator class for design and quality. Claude Code: 1. Design issue (Calculator.java:5-45) All methods are static. This prevents testing with mocks and violates OOP principles. Consider instance methods. Provenance: documented (Google Java Style Guide §3.4) 2. Missing validation (Calculator.java:25) modulo(a, b) doesn’t check b == 0. Will throw ArithmeticException with no useful message. Provenance: verified (code inspection) 3. Logging gap (Calculator.java:18) divide() uses System.out.println() instead of SLF4J. Provenance: documented (CLAUDE.md rule: use SLF4J) 4. Naming (Calculator.java:31) Parameter "a" and "b" — non-descriptive. Use "dividend" and "divisor" for domain clarity. Provenance: inferred (readability best practice) 5. Architecture (Calculator.java) Business logic mixed with I/O (readLine in main). Separate input handling from calculation logic. Provenance: documented (layered architecture pattern) Summary: 1 design, 1 bug, 1 convention, 1 naming, 1 architecture.
body.note: 5 findings across 4 provenance types. Static tools catch #3 (logging). Only AI catches #1 (design), #2 (missing guard), #5 (architecture).

items: Claude Code suggests a refactoring. You apply it. The code looks better.
But did you break anything?

body.code: # Before refactoring mvn test # all green # Apply AI suggestion... # After refactoring mvn test # still green? Then it’s safe.

body.quote: Rule: Always run tests after AI-suggested refactoring.

items: Add CheckStyle, PMD, and SpotBugs plugins to your pom.xml
Use Claude Code: "Add quality plugins for CheckStyle (google_checks), PMD, and SpotBugs"
Run: mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check spotbugs:check
Verify: each tool produces output (even if violations = 0)
Intentionally add a bad method: public void x(){ }
Run again — which tool catches what?

body.code: <!-- Minimum pom.xml plugins block to add: --> <build><plugins> <!-- CheckStyle --> <plugin> <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId> <artifactId>maven-checkstyle-plugin</artifactId> <version>3.3.1</version> <configuration><configLocation>google_checks.xml</configLocation> <failOnViolation>true</failOnViolation></configuration> </plugin> <!-- PMD + SpotBugs: same pattern, ask Claude Code --> </plugins></build>
body.note: CheckStyle config shown above. For PMD and SpotBugs, ask Claude Code to add them — then verify the versions on search.maven.org.

body.card: Task: Add this intentionally bad code:
items: public String describe(Object obj) { return obj.toString(); } — What if obj is null?
FileReader reader = new FileReader(path); — never closes reader
Run SpotBugs — what does it find?
Run PMD — what does it find?
Which tool found which bug?
Fix both bugs
Run both tools again to confirm
Ask Claude Code: "Review my Calculator class for quality issues"
Run: mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check spotbugs:check
Fill in the comparison table below
Discuss with a classmate: which approach found more? Which found deeper issues?

table.note: Key insight: static tools catch rules. AI catches intent. Neither catches everything alone.

title: cognitive-core: /pre-commit Skill
desc: Structured pass/fail report before every commit.

body.code: /pre-commit Pre-commit check for 3 staged files: File Lint Format Status ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── Calculator.java PASS PASS OK CalculatorTest.java PASS PASS OK GradeManager.java FAIL PASS BLOCKED ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── Result: 1 file blocked. Fix violations, then retry. GradeManager.java:42 — PMD: empty catch block GradeManager.java:67 — CheckStyle: line too long (142 > 100)
body.note: The skill checks only staged files (git diff --cached). You fix the violations, re-stage, and run /pre-commit again until all files pass.

title: Clean Logging: SLF4J Instead of System.out
desc: System.out.println() is not logging. It is debugging that stayed in production.

body.code: // WRONG — not logging System.out.println("User logged in: " + username); e.printStackTrace(); // RIGHT — proper logging import org.slf4j.Logger; import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory; private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(Calculator.class); log.info("User logged in: {}", username); log.error("Calculation failed", exception);

items: Log levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR — filter what you see
Configuration: change output without changing code
Files: logs go to files, not just console
Production: System.out disappears in server environments

body.quote: ⚠️ System.out.println() or e.printStackTrace() in your project costs -0.3 on your grade. Use SLF4J.

title: The Vibe Coding Experiment
desc: What happens when you let AI code without constraints?

items: Use Claude Code with zero configuration — no CLAUDE.md, no rules, no constraints
Task: “Add a complete new feature to your project”
Commit the unreviewed AI-generated code to a vibe-coding-experiment branch
Then count the damage: run mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check spotbugs:check
Post your violation count on the whiteboard

body.quote: This is the most important lesson of the course: unconstrained AI produces defects at scale.

title: The Data: Unconstrained AI Code Quality
desc: CodeRabbit 2025 study — analyzing millions of AI-generated pull requests.

rows: Doubled — AI code gets rewritten or reverted more often
2.74× more in AI-generated pull requests
Only 65.2% of Copilot suggestions were correct in study tasks
8× more performance problems than human code
AI tests have lower mutation scores — they pass but miss real bugs
Moved code and copy-pasted code increased significantly
table.note: Multiple independent studies, same conclusion: AI-generated code needs review and quality gates.

items: AI has no project context — it does not know your coding standards
AI optimizes for “looks correct”, not for “is correct”
AI generates code fast — more code = more surface area for bugs
AI does not run tests — it cannot verify its own output (unless it is an agent)

body.quote: AI writes fast. Fast is not the same as good. That is why you need quality gates — they catch what AI misses.

title: CLAUDE.md: Teaching AI Your Project Rules
desc: The single most important file for AI-assisted development.

body.code: # CLAUDE.md — project root ## Project Java 21 Maven project: Student Grade Manager ## Coding Standards - Follow google_checks.xml (CheckStyle) - Use SLF4J for logging, NEVER System.out.println() - All public methods must have Javadoc ## Forbidden Patterns - NEVER use e.printStackTrace() - NEVER use wildcard imports - NEVER suppress warnings without justification
body.note: Claude Code reads this file automatically. It becomes the AI’s project context.

items: AI might ignore your rules — especially under complex prompts or long conversations
AI might use alternatives — e.g. Logback instead of SLF4J, JUnit 4 instead of 5
AI cannot be trusted to follow rules 100% of the time — it is probabilistic, not deterministic
That is why you still need CheckStyle, PMD, SpotBugs — they enforce rules the AI forgets

title: Custom Rules and Commands
desc: Fine-grained control over AI behavior in your project.

body.code: # .claude/rules/java-conventions.md --- globs: "src/**/*.java" --- - Google Java Style Guide (2-space indent) - SLF4J + LoggerFactory, never System.out - Exception handling: catch specific, log with context - JUnit 5 with @DisplayName on every test
body.note: Rules in .claude/rules/ activate only for matching file patterns.

body.code: # .claude/commands/quality-check.md Run the full quality pipeline: 1. mvn checkstyle:check 2. mvn pmd:check 3. mvn spotbugs:check Report violations grouped by tool. Suggest fixes for each violation.
body.note: Custom slash commands: type /project:quality-check in Claude Code.

title: Built-in Tools: What Claude Code Can Do
desc: Understanding the agent’s capabilities helps you use it effectively.

rows: Read any file in your project
table.note: Claude Code asks for approval before running high-risk tools. This is the tool gateway pattern from Module 6.

items: Create CLAUDE.md with your project rules
Add .claude/rules/java-conventions.md
Ask Claude Code to add the same feature again
Run mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check spotbugs:check
Compare violation counts: before vs. after

table.note: Constraints don’t slow AI down. They make AI output usable.

title: Exercise 3.4: Vibe Coding vs. Constrained AI

items: Create branch vibe-coding-experiment
Ask Claude Code (no CLAUDE.md): “Add a statistics feature that calculates median, mode, and standard deviation”
Commit the raw output. Run all quality tools. Write down violation counts.
Create branch constrained-experiment
Add CLAUDE.md and .claude/rules/ with your quality standards
Ask for the same feature. Run quality tools. Compare counts.

body.quote: Part 3 (5 min): Post both numbers on the whiteboard. Class discussion: what changed and why?

items: Run: bash install.sh from the cognitive-core directory
Select platform: claude
Verify: .claude/hooks/ directory was created
Verify: cognitive-core.conf exists in your project
Edit any .java file — watch post-edit-lint fire automatically
Try: git push --force main — observe the BLOCKED message
Try writing a hardcoded password in code — observe validate-write block it
Run: /pre-commit — see pass/fail per staged file
Run: /fitness — see your 4-category score vs. thresholds
Run: /code-review — read the structured findings with provenance
Compare: which found more — mvn checkstyle:check or /code-review?
CheckStyle = formatting and style consistency
PMD = bad patterns and code smells
SpotBugs = actual bugs in compiled bytecode
Configure tools to fail the build on violations
Claude Code goes beyond rules — understands design and intent
Always run tests after refactoring (even AI-suggested)
Industry: quality checks that block builds = regression gates in production systems
Use tools for automated gates, AI for deeper review
